text:
[CFB-INDEX-VOICE]
# Identity and heritage

Michigan football was founded in 1879 — older than every Big Ten program, older than the conference itself. The program has more all-time wins than any other in college football history (1,020+ through 2024) and the highest all-time win percentage (.728) among programs with 1,000+ games. Twelve national championships are claimed, including 1901–1904 (the Yost "Point-a-Minute" teams), 1918, 1923, 1932, 1933, 1947, 1948, 1997, and 2023. The 2023 title — Jim Harbaugh's final year, the program's first CFP championship, the culmination of a three-year run of Ohio State wins — is the modern spine.

Michigan Stadium — "The Big House" — opened in 1927 and has been expanded multiple times to its current 107,601 capacity, the largest in the Western Hemisphere. Saturday attendance regularly exceeds 110,000. The maize-and-blue winged helmet (adopted 1938) is one of college football's most iconic visuals. The fight song, "The Victors," was written in 1898 and called by John Philip Sousa "the greatest college fight song ever written" — a Sousa quote Michigan fans have not let die.

The program's alma mater contains the line "the leaders and best," which is the program's self-definition in three words: institutional, aspirational, and unabashed about claiming the spot.

# Coaching lineage

Fielding Yost (1901–1926) built the first dynasty — the "Point-a-Minute" teams of 1901–1905 that went 55-1-1, outscoring opponents 2,821-42. Yost also coached the 1923 national champion team and built Michigan Stadium. Fritz Crisler (1938–1947) added the 1947 title and the winged helmet. Bennie Oosterbaan (1948–1958) added the 1948 title.

Bo Schembechler (1969–1989) is the program's cornerstone — 194-48-5, 13 Big Ten titles, no national championship but 17 straight winning seasons. Schembechler's "Those who stay will be champions" locker-room speech (1969) is the program's most-cited internal text. His "The Team. The Team. The Team." speech (1983) is its most-cited external text. Schembechler died in 2006, one day before The Game.

Gary Moeller (1990–1994) and Lloyd Carr (1995–2007) continued. Carr won the 1997 national title with Charles Woodson and led the program through the late-90s / 2000s peak. The post-Carr wilderness (Rich Rodriguez 2008–2010, Brady Hoke 2011–2014) was the program's darkest modern era — three years of Rodriguez at 15-22, Hoke at 31-20 but losing the Ohio State rivalry thoroughly.

Jim Harbaugh (2015–2023) came home to Ann Arbor and delivered the 2023 national title — Michigan's first in 26 years. Harbaugh left for the Los Angeles Chargers after the title; his final Michigan game was the 34-13 win over Washington in the CFP National Championship. Sherrone Moore (2024–present), Harbaugh's promoted offensive coordinator, inherits the program post-title with the sign-stealing NCAA investigation as the open cloud.

# Notable players

Heismans: Tom Harmon (1940), Desmond Howard (1991), Charles Woodson (1997 — the only primarily-defensive Heisman winner). Pre-war: Bob Chappuis. Bo era: Anthony Carter, Rob Lytle, Harlan Huckleby. Carr era: Tom Brady, Charles Woodson, Lamarr Woodley, Mike Hart. Modern era: Denard Robinson, Jabrill Peppers, Aidan Hutchinson, Hassan Haskins, Blake Corum (2022 Heisman finalist), J.J. McCarthy, Donovan Edwards, Roman Wilson, Will Johnson, Colston Loveland.

The 2023 national champion roster sent five first-rounders to the 2024 NFL Draft.

# Fans and culture

The fanbase is dense in Michigan state + strong in the Detroit and Chicago corridors + a national alumni diaspora that runs through every major market. "Michigan Men" (Schembechler's term for Michigan alumni) is the fanbase's self-label. The gameday register is "largest-venue-in-America intensity without losing composure" — loud but not rowdy, expectant but not boisterous. The "Hail to the Victors" fight song is played after every score; the crowd sings along with "Hail to the Victors / Hail to the conquering heroes / Hail, Hail to Michigan / The leaders and best."

Pre-game traditions include the team touching the "Go Blue" banner as they run onto the field, the marching band's M Block formation, "The Victors" before kickoff. The stadium's actual capacity is deliberately listed as "109,901 + 1" on the scoreboard — the +1 is for Fritz Crisler. At the end of the third quarter the crowd stretches and the stadium plays "Mr. Brightside" — a millennial tradition that has spread across college football.

The internal fracture most visible post-Harbaugh is generational and succession-focused: older fans trust the institution; younger fans expect the wins to keep coming under Moore. The NCAA sign-stealing investigation is the open operational cloud.

# Voice and ethos

The voice is proud-institutional: confident, historic, allergic to both underdog framings and pageantry. Michigan speaks about itself in the register of "the program that has been doing this longer than anyone" without chest-thumping about it. The specific Michigan register is dry — close to an alumni magazine — and uninterested in performative swagger.

Identity phrase: "Michigan is the nation's winningest program, and still plays like the total isn't finished."

Mantra: "Go Blue." Deploy as greeting, sign-off, or ambient affirmation. Fans will respond "Go Blue" without prompting.

Stock phrases — "Those who stay will be champions," "The Team. The Team. The Team.," "Leaders and best," "Michigan against everybody" — are load-bearing. Each has specific historical weight (Schembechler speeches, the alma mater, Harbaugh-era siege rhetoric). Use where earned.

The never-use list excludes frames that diminish institutional stature ("midwestern afterthought," "plucky"), frames that lean too hard into the sign-stealing scandal as the program's identity, and frames that treat the 2023 national title as a fluke rather than the earned outcome of a three-year Harbaugh run. "Cute Michigan" — any register that would frame Michigan with small-program warmth — is wrong.

# Rivalries

The Game (vs. Ohio State, annual since 1918) is the program's defining rivalry and the defining rivalry in American college football. Michigan currently leads the all-time series 60-51-6 (through 2024). The Bo era's rivalry peak is remembered as the modern golden era (Bo vs. Hayes, then Bo vs. Earle Bruce, then Cooper era Ohio State struggles). The 2021–2023 Michigan three-peat — after losing 8 in a row to OSU — was the emotional release that powered the 2023 title run. The 2024 Ohio State win interrupted the streak and lit OSU's title run.

Michigan–Michigan State (Paul Bunyan Trophy, annual) is the in-state rivalry. Dominated historically by Michigan; briefly competitive under Dantonio's Michigan State. The 2015 punt game (Michigan State's blocked punt for the winning TD) is a canonical MSU moment and a Michigan wound.

Michigan–Notre Dame was annual through the 2010s, hiatused after 2014, resumed in 2018. Historically significant; emotional weight calibrated.

Michigan–Penn State has grown in weight since Penn State's Big Ten addition (1993). Modern era defined by the 2022 Harbaugh-Franklin wins and the 2016 PSU win. Now a top-3 Big Ten rivalry by importance.

# Current context

Head coach: Sherrone Moore (3rd year, 2026). Offensive coordinator: Kirk Campbell. Defensive coordinator: Don "Wink" Martindale. Athletic director: Warde Manuel. NIL collective: Champions Circle. Facilities: Schembechler Hall, the Big House expansion projects, the Stephen M. Ross Athletic Campus.

Conference: Big Ten (post-realignment structure; 18 teams in 2024+).

# Program narratives

The 2023 national championship closed a 26-year title drought and vindicated the Harbaugh hire. The 2024 post-Harbaugh transition under Moore was a 7-5 recalibration; 2025 improved to 10-3; 2026 is the referendum year on whether Moore is a multi-decade coach or a bridge.

The NCAA sign-stealing investigation (centered on the 2023 season) is a real operational cloud. The program and the NCAA reached a finding in 2025 that involved scholarship reductions and a suspension for Moore but no title vacatur. The fanbase's register on the scandal: dismissive of outside moralism, defensive of the program's results, wary of future sanctions.

The portal and NIL era have favored Michigan at a modest scale compared to SEC peers; the program's traditional in-state and regional pipeline remains strong but the high-end NIL race is being contested.

# Aspiration framework

Baseline: Big Ten Championship Game and CFP bid. Realistic ceiling: CFP semifinal and national title game. Dream ceiling: back-to-back national titles (first since 1947-48). Historic ceiling: the 1901–1904 Yost Point-a-Minute run, the 1997 perfect season, or the 2023 championship. Historic floor: 2008 (3-9) under Rodriguez. Unlock: contender view is always on.

# Chronicle tuning

For Michigan, the anomalies that resonate are Bo-era or Harbaugh-era relative: a defensive points-allowed figure matched to the 2023 title team is the frame that lands. Echoes resonate when connected to Yost (the founding dynasty), Bo (the cultural spine), Carr '97 (the last prior title), Harbaugh '23 (the most recent). Retroactive patterns: "Michigan in close games vs. Ohio State," "Fourth-quarter defense in Big Ten title games," "Harbaugh-era comebacks."

# In-jokes and copypasta

"Those who stay will be champions" — Schembechler, 1969; universally known. "The Team. The Team. The Team." — Schembechler, 1983; deployed as an exhortation or a rebuke. "Beat Ohio" / "Beat Ohio State" — the annual directive. "The Victors" (fight song) — sung after every score. "Maize and blue" — color identity. "M Go Blue" — fanbase shorthand. "Mr. Brightside" — third-quarter crowd tradition. "Harbaugh khakis" — the specific beige pants Harbaugh wore on the sideline; an in-joke of the Harbaugh era.

# Taboos and sensitivities

The 2023 sign-stealing investigation is the open wound — handled honestly, not dismissed, not dwelled on. The Rodriguez and Hoke eras (2008–2014) are program wilderness — referenced historically, not dwelt on. The 2015 punt game vs. Michigan State is a recurring painful moment; "the punt" is Michigan shorthand and carries weight. The "khakis" jokes about Harbaugh run close to affection and parody; use carefully. The death of Schembechler in 2006 — one day before The Game — is a program touchstone, invoked reverently.

[CFB-INDEX-VOICE]
# Identity and heritage

USC football was founded in 1888 — one of the oldest continuously-operating programs on the West Coast. Eleven national championships are claimed (1928, 1931, 1932, 1939, 1962, 1967, 1972, 1974, 1978, 2003, 2004). The 2004 title, vacated in 2010 as part of the Reggie Bush NCAA sanctions, was restored in 2024 when the NCAA's recent enforcement reform returned the title to the program's record. Seven Heisman Trophy winners (the 1965, 1968, 1979, 1981, 2002, 2004, 2005 recipients) — a count second only to Notre Dame's among all programs. Twelve NFL Hall of Famers — more than any other college program.

The Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum — home field since 1923 — has hosted three Olympic Games (1932, 1984, upcoming 2028), two Super Bowls, and dozens of heavyweight college football games. The Coliseum's historic register is double: it's one of American college football's most storied venues and one of Los Angeles's civic landmarks. Renovated in 2019 with a $315M project funded by United Airlines naming rights.

"Fight On" — the hand sign and the mantra — has been a program identity element since the early 20th century. "Conquest" — the post-victory celebration song — is canonical. The Song Girls (the cheer/dance squad), the Trojan warrior mascot, and the white horse "Traveler" (the Trojan Horse stand-in, ridden onto the field after every home score) are the program's gameday visual signatures.

# Coaching lineage

Howard Jones (1925–1940) established USC as a national power with four national titles and three Rose Bowl wins. Jeff Cravath and Jess Hill bridged the program through mid-century. John McKay (1960–1975) is the program's cornerstone — 127-40-8, four national titles (1962, 1967, 1972, 1974), and five Heisman winners in his tenure. McKay's power-I running attack and his "Tailback U" mythology define the program's classical identity.

John Robinson I (1976–1982) added the 1978 national title and two Heismans (Charles White '79, Marcus Allen '81). The program waxed and waned through the 1980s–1990s. Pete Carroll (2001–2009) built the modern dynasty: 97-19 record, two national titles (2003 AP, 2004 BCS), 34-game win streak, Carson Palmer / Matt Leinart / Reggie Bush Heisman trio. Carroll left for the Seattle Seahawks in early 2010; the NCAA sanctions (for Bush impermissible benefits) followed shortly after — two-year bowl ban, 30 scholarship reductions, vacated wins, and the 2004 national title vacatur (restored 2024).

The post-sanctions wilderness (Lane Kiffin 2010–2013, Steve Sarkisian 2014–2015, Clay Helton 2015–2021) produced one Pac-12 title (2017) but no serious national championship contention across more than a decade. Lincoln Riley (2022–present), the former Oklahoma head coach, has produced 11-3, 7-6, 9-4 through his first three seasons. The 2024 season — Riley's third, the first year in the Big Ten — was the program's highest-pressure year since the Carroll era.

# Notable players

Heismans: Mike Garrett (1965), O.J. Simpson (1968), Charles White (1979), Marcus Allen (1981), Carson Palmer (2002), Matt Leinart (2004), Reggie Bush (2005). The Bush Heisman was vacated in 2010 and restored in 2024 — he is now officially a USC Heisman winner again.

Tailback lineage: Simpson, Anthony Davis, Ricky Bell, White, Allen, LenDale White, Reggie Bush — "Tailback U." Modern era: Sam Darnold, Kedon Slovis, Caleb Williams (2022 Heisman — at USC as a Riley-transfer from Oklahoma), Jayden Maiava, Zachariah Branch. The program has produced 85+ NFL draft picks since 2000.

# Fans and culture

The fanbase is Southern California-concentrated with a national alumni diaspora — LA/Orange County saturation, a strong Bay Area presence, and a national network built on the Carroll dynasty's early-2000s ratings peak. The gameday register is "Hollywood-dynastic" in a specific way: the Coliseum at kickoff is a celebrity-spotting event layered over a serious football environment. Traveler gallops after every score. The Song Girls execute their classical routines. The band plays "Conquest" with increasing frequency as the game's margin builds.

"Fight On" is the universal greeting. The Trojan warrior (Tommy Trojan statue on campus; helmet-and-plume mascot) is the iconography. Cardinal and gold as color identity is SoCal-embedded — the USC flag flies over dozens of alumni-owned businesses across LA. "SC" as vertical shorthand is fan-internal.

The internal fracture most visible: the older fans who lived through the Carroll dynasty and treat any post-2009 USC team as failing relative to that standard, and the younger fans who know only the wilderness years and see the 2024–25 Riley era as legitimate progress. The sanctions era's long tail on the fanbase — the felt injustice of the Bush vacatur — produced a specific USC fan personality ("we were wronged") that has become less defining with the 2024 restoration but remains part of institutional memory.

# Voice and ethos

The voice is Hollywood-dynastic: institutional, pedigreed, SoCal-specific, allergic to both underdog framings and to being treated as a California punchline. USC does not speak as a plucky program; it speaks as one of American college football's half-dozen all-time programs that happens to have had a rough fifteen-year stretch. The register is aware of the stretch but does not apologize for or center it.

Identity phrase: "USC is the program that used to own Saturday night, and is trying to remember how it felt."

Mantra: "Fight On." With the hand sign (V shape, two fingers). Used as greeting, sign-off, celebratory punctuation.

Stock phrases — "Fight On," "Tailback U," "Cardinal and gold," "Conquest" — have specific historical weight and should appear where earned. "The Ball Coach" is John McKay's nickname; historical reference only. "The Song Girls" are a specific part of the program's visual identity.

The never-use list excludes frames that reduce USC to stereotype ("Hollywood" as a punchline, "USC kids show up in uniforms from central casting"). "Scrappy" and "plucky" are wrong for USC; the program's plucky period was Homer's Iliad. The Reggie Bush sanctions are part of the record but the title was restored in 2024 — framing the program's identity around the vacated title reads as stuck in a resolved chapter.

# Rivalries

USC–Notre Dame (Jeweled Shillelagh) is the program's national-identity rivalry. Played annually since 1926 (with pandemic and war disruptions). The 2005 "Bush Push" game is canonical. Both programs now in different conferences but the series continues as a non-conference annual. The 2024 Notre Dame win (Leonard's triple-OT keeper) was a brutal Trojan loss.

USC–UCLA (Victory Bell) is the crosstown rivalry. Both programs share the LA market; the Victory Bell trophy has alternated since 1942. Both joined the Big Ten in 2024, making the rivalry a conference game. "Coliseum or Rose Bowl" alternation continues.

USC–Stanford and USC–California were the Pac-12 era staples; both dormant post-realignment.

USC–Washington has emerged as a Big Ten rivalry; the 2024 Washington win at Husky Stadium was one of the conference's signature results.

# Current context

Head coach: Lincoln Riley (5th year, 2026). Offensive coordinator: Riley retains play-calling. Defensive coordinator: D'Anton Lynn. Athletic director: Jennifer Cohen (hired 2023, formerly at Washington). NIL collective: House of Victory (launched 2022). Facilities: John McKay Center (2012 reno), Coliseum renovation (2019).

Conference: Big Ten (since 2024). Previous: Pac-12 (and PCC / Pac-8/10 before).

# Program narratives

The Lincoln Riley restoration is the central open narrative. Hired in late 2021 with universal acclamation (the former Oklahoma head coach moving to the Coliseum), Riley's first three years produced progress (11-3 in 2022) and stalls (7-6 in 2023) before a 9-4 2024 / 10-3 2025 ascending pair. The 2026 question: is Riley the long-arc answer or a multi-year bridge?

The 2004 title restoration (2024) was a rare piece of institutional good news. Reggie Bush's Heisman back in the trophy case is a fanbase catharsis moment that has not yet fully been priced into the program's voice register.

The portal and NIL era have favored USC in the Riley years — Caleb Williams' Heisman run (2022, at USC after Oklahoma) was the CFB-era's most high-profile portal success story. The program's ongoing recruiting competitiveness with Oregon, Alabama, Ohio State, and Texas is the 2026 question.

# Aspiration framework

Baseline: Big Ten Championship Game appearance and CFP bid. Realistic ceiling: CFP semifinal. Dream ceiling: national championship #12 (the first since 2004). Historic ceiling: the 2003–2005 Carroll peak, the 1972 national champion team, the 1978 title season. Historic floor: 2021 (4-8) under Helton. Unlock: contender view is always on.

# Chronicle tuning

For USC, the anomaly that resonates is Carroll-era relative: a points-per-game figure compared to the 2003 or 2005 team reads as the frame. Echoes resonate when connected to the Carroll dynasty, the McKay era, the Jones era, or the 1978 title team. Retroactive patterns: "USC at the Coliseum under the lights" (historically dominant), "USC vs Notre Dame in October" (canonical), "Tailback U production" (the historical rushing tradition).

# In-jokes and copypasta

"Fight On" — universal; hand sign is V-for-victory. "Tailback U" — claim, accepted by most historians. "Conquest" — the post-victory song; triggers emotional response. "Reggie's Heisman" — the 2024 restoration is still fanbase catharsis material. "Bush Push" — the 2005 Notre Dame game; canonical. "The Song Girls" — proper noun. "SC" vertical shorthand — fan-internal. "Cardinal and gold" — color identity.

# Taboos and sensitivities

The 2005 Rose Bowl loss to Texas (Vince Young 4th-down run, 41-38) is a wound. The 2010 NCAA sanctions era — specifically the Bush vacatur before the 2024 restoration — is sensitive; the restoration resolved much but the 2010-2014 competitive damage is real. The Lane Kiffin LSU-style "airport firing" (2013) is historical curiosity. Reggie Bush's restored Heisman is a joy, not a cautionary tale. The 2024 Notre Dame triple-OT loss (Leonard's run) is the program's most recent acute wound.

[CFB-INDEX-VOICE]
# Identity and heritage

Ohio State football began in 1890 and has since accumulated what is arguably the Midwest's most load-bearing football identity. Eight national championships are claimed and generally accepted (1942, 1954, 1957, 1961, 1968, 1970, 2002, 2014), with the 2024 CFP national championship adding a ninth. Seven Heismans — Les Horvath, Vic Janowicz, Hopalong Cassady, Archie Griffin (twice, the only two-time Heisman winner), Eddie George, Troy Smith. Forty-two Big Ten titles claimed, by far the conference's richest haul.

Ohio Stadium — universally called "The Horseshoe" — opened in 1922 and now seats 102,780, making it one of the largest on-campus venues in America. The building itself is a National Historic Landmark. The marching band's Script Ohio formation, performed pregame at home games since 1936, is a program icon that transcends football — a living piece of midwestern cultural identity that happens to exist inside a football institution. "Hang on Sloopy" is played between the third and fourth quarters. "Carmen Ohio" is the alma mater sung after every home game, win or lose. These are not peripheral rituals; they are the program.

# Coaching lineage

The cornerstone is Woody Hayes (1951–1978), whose 205-61-10 record, three national titles, and 13 Big Ten championships built the program's modern identity. Hayes' "three yards and a cloud of dust" football was its own aesthetic argument; his firing in 1978 (for striking a Clemson player at the Gator Bowl) was its own final chapter. Earle Bruce (1979–1987) steadied the program. John Cooper (1988–2000) produced strong records but lost the Michigan rivalry at an unacceptable rate (2-10-1 vs. Michigan) and was fired despite 111 wins.

Jim Tressel (2001–2010) won the 2002 national title — the program's first since 1968 — with a defensive-minded system and a 9-1 record against Michigan. Tressel's tenure ended in NCAA sanctions (the "tattoo" scandal). Urban Meyer (2012–2018) rebuilt the program at an accelerated pace, going 83-9 in seven seasons and winning the 2014 national title. Meyer's departure amid a domestic-violence handling scandal is a real piece of the recent record but not the defining one.

Ryan Day (2019–present) inherited a championship-caliber program and spent 2021–2023 as the first Buckeyes coach in a long time to lose three straight to Michigan, which triggered fan discontent that reached a peak after the 2023 Michigan loss. The 2024 season — beating Michigan, winning the CFP quarterfinal/semifinal/final, claiming the ninth national title — resolved that discontent on the field while leaving real institutional questions about the Day era's trajectory going forward.

# Notable players

Heismans: Les Horvath (1944), Vic Janowicz (1950), Hopalong Cassady (1955), Archie Griffin (1974 and 1975 — the only two-time winner), Eddie George (1995), Troy Smith (2006). Modern era: Chase Young, Joey Bosa, Nick Bosa, Justin Fields, C.J. Stroud, Marvin Harrison Jr., TreVeyon Henderson, Will Howard (2024 title-winning QB, transfer from Kansas State). The program's NFL pipeline has been sustained over eras; the 2023 and 2024 drafts produced first-round selections at multiple positions.

# Fans and culture

The fanbase is the most numerically large Big Ten fan base and one of the top-three most engaged in college football by any revealed-preference measure (TV ratings, merch, road-game presence). Geography is heavily Ohio-concentrated with a strong Columbus/Cleveland/Cincinnati triangle plus statewide saturation, plus a national diaspora concentrated in coastal alumni cities.

The gameday register is industrial-scarlet: not sentimental, not performatively rowdy, but loud, consistent, and structurally integrated into Columbus's civic life in a way few college towns experience. The "O-H / I-O" call-and-response is a greeting as much as a cheer — it operates in airports, grocery stores, and business meetings. Script Ohio is watched with reverence by locals and awe by visitors. The fanbase's internal register is confidence without pageantry; the expectation of winning is institutional, not performative.

The internal fracture most visible during the 2021–2023 stretch was generational: older fans who experienced the Cooper-era frustration of losing to Michigan saw the Day-era losses as intolerable echoes; younger fans who grew up in the Tressel/Meyer dominance saw them as aberrations. The 2024 title resolved the argument on the field.

# Voice and ethos

The voice is dynastic-industrial: precise, unsentimental, institutional, allergic to both underdog framings and pageantry. Ohio State does not speak about its success as a feel-good story or a cultural pageant; the program speaks about itself the way a manufacturing concern speaks about its operating rigor. "The standard is the standard" — the phrase Meyer operationalized — is the register in one line.

Identity phrase: "Ohio State is the Midwest's standing answer — the program that does not ask permission to be in the conversation."

Mantra: "Go Bucks." The greeting is "O-H," the response "I-O." Deploy the mantra and the call-and-response where earned; overuse collapses them into the ambient register of fan copy.

Stock phrases — "The standard is the standard," "Beat Michigan," "Script Ohio is a statement of purpose" — land when they land. The program is direct; its copy should be too.

The never-use list is explicit. "Plucky," "Cinderella," "scrappy" are for other programs. "Big Ten-slow" as a pejorative is a rival's line. Reductive framings of Urban Meyer (his scandal is a fact of the record but is not the program's identity) or of Ryan Day (the 2021–2023 Michigan losses are real but not the totality of his record) read as outside-copy and miss what the fanbase actually thinks. "Flyover team" is outside-coastal-media copy that the program does not accept.

# Rivalries

The Game (vs. Michigan, annual since 1918) is the defining rivalry in American college football. The all-time series currently sits at 60-51-6 Michigan (through 2024). The 2021, 2022, and 2023 Michigan wins were the program's most painful recent stretch; the 2024 Ohio State win — which ignited the championship run — was the relief release. The game is played the last Saturday of the regular season. Ohio State's calendar is organized around it.

Ohio State–Penn State is the Big Ten's other top-tier rivalry. Annual, conference-championship-adjacent, and host to the "White Out" game at Beaver Stadium — one of the loudest environments in college football. Ohio State has generally held the series edge in the CFP era; Penn State has produced the most competitive close games.

Ohio State–Michigan State defined the 2011–2015 Big Ten competitive stretch under Mark Dantonio. The Land Grant Trophy is the trophy; the rivalry has cooled since but remains on the calendar.

# Current context

Head coach: Ryan Day (7th season, 2026). Offensive coordinator: Brian Hartline. Defensive coordinator: Jim Knowles' successor (changes tracked). Athletic director: Ross Bjork (arrived 2024 from Texas A&M). NIL collective: The Foundation / Ohio Collective. Facilities: Woody Hayes Athletic Center + the 2019 renovation of the Schottenstein Center / Ohio Stadium ongoing projects.

Conference: Big Ten (East division through 2023; no-division format from 2024 onward). Scheduling: nine Big Ten games annually plus a Power-conference non-conference game (e.g., Notre Dame, Texas, Oregon in alternating years).

# Program narratives

The 2024 national championship is the near-term narrative center of gravity. A program that spent two years answering "can Ryan Day beat Michigan / win the big one" answered both in one season. The 2025 season's task was defending that altitude; 2026 is the question of whether the Day era is a Tressel-like multi-title arc or a 2014-Meyer-like singular peak.

The portal and NIL era continue to reshape roster construction. Ohio State has been an early adopter of high-end portal investments — Will Howard in 2024, the continuing QB pipeline — and the fanbase's tolerance for portal dependence is still being established.

# Aspiration framework

Baseline: Big Ten Championship Game appearance, CFP top-4 seed, 11+ wins. Realistic ceiling: CFP semifinal and national title game. Dream ceiling: back-to-back national championships (which would be the first in the CFP era for any program). Historic ceiling: the Griffin-era dominance of the mid-1970s or the 2014 run. Historic floor: 1988 (4-6-1) or 1999 (6-6) — long-ago stretches that the current fanbase has essentially no institutional memory of experiencing.

# Chronicle tuning

For Ohio State, the anomaly that reads loudest is any stat that distinguishes current roster production from Meyer-era or Tressel-era baselines — the program's historical library is deep enough that almost any number has a comp. Echoes resonate when they connect to 2014 (the first CFP title), 2002 (Tressel's first title), 1968 (the Hayes peak), or the 1975 Griffin Heisman repeat. Retroactive cards work best when tied to Michigan-week performance or CFP elimination games.

# In-jokes and copypasta

"O-H / I-O" — the universal call and response; sincere, not a bit. "The Team The Team The Team" — this is Bo Schembechler's Michigan line; Ohio State does not claim it. "Script Ohio" — a proper noun on the program's identity. "Hang on Sloopy" — mid-third-quarter tradition; sacred. "Carmen Ohio" — sung after home games regardless of result. "Beat Michigan" — a direction, not a slogan.

# Taboos and sensitivities

Woody Hayes' 1978 Gator Bowl firing (striking a Clemson player) is historical fact, handled with context. Jim Tressel's NCAA sanctions (2011) and Urban Meyer's 2018 scandal are part of the record; neither is the program's defining story, but dismissing either is patronizing. The 2021–2023 Michigan losses are three wounds — acknowledged, not dwelt on. Ryan Day's "can he win the big one" narrative was resolved in 2024; copy that continues to re-litigate it after that moment reads as stuck. The 2016 CFP loss to Clemson (31-0) and the 2019 CFP loss to Clemson (29-23) are recent wounds — survivable references, not punchlines.

[CFB-INDEX-VOICE]
# Identity and heritage

Texas football was founded in 1893 and has operated since as one of American college football's flagship state-university programs. Four national championships: 1963, 1969, 1970, and 2005. Two Heismans: Earl Campbell (1977) and Ricky Williams (1998). Thirty-three conference championships across Southwest Conference (1915–1995) and Big 12 (1996–2023) play. In 2024 Texas joined the SEC, alongside Oklahoma, as part of the conference realignment that reshaped American college football's power structure.

Darrell K Royal-Texas Memorial Stadium — "DKR" — opened 1924 and now holds 100,119 after multiple expansions. Named for the program's most important coach; his statue stands outside the stadium. Bevo — the live Texas longhorn steer mascot — has been a continuous line since 1916, with the current Bevo being the fifteenth. The program's colors, burnt orange and white, are one of the most recognizable identity palettes in American college sport.

The 2005 national championship — Vince Young's 8-yard fourth-down run vs. USC in the Rose Bowl with 19 seconds left — is the modern spine of the program's identity. It's also the line that divides recent Texas history: everything before is context for the title; everything after is measured against the decade-and-a-half of not being able to get back.

# Coaching lineage

Dana X. Bible (1937–1946) stabilized the pre-war program. Darrell K. Royal (1957–1976) is the program's cornerstone — 167-47-5 record, three national titles (1963, 1969, 1970), 11 SWC championships. Royal's "wishbone" offense, designed with Emory Bellard, revolutionized college football. Royal's statue, DKR stadium, and the program's cultural memory all anchor to him. He died in 2012; his legacy endures.

The post-Royal wilderness (Fred Akers, David McWilliams, John Mackovic — 1977–1997) produced three SWC titles and a Heisman (Campbell 1977) but no national championships and only one Cotton Bowl win in the final SWC decade. Mack Brown (1998–2013) restored the program: 158-48 record, two BCS title-game appearances, the 2005 national championship, Ricky Williams' Heisman (1998), and a decade (1998–2009) of sustained top-10 presence.

The collapse era (Charlie Strong 2014–2016, Tom Herman 2017–2020) is the program's defining recent wound. Seven years of losing seasons in a span where Oklahoma dominated the Big 12 and the SEC pulled away as the national power conference. The "Texas is back" meme — born in this era from each false restart — still hangs over the program.

Steve Sarkisian (2021–present) inherited a damaged program, produced three losing-to-competitive years (2021 5-7, 2022 8-5, 2023 12-2), and delivered a 2024 SEC-first-season 13-3 run that reached the CFP semifinal. 2026 is Sarkisian's sixth year and the question of whether Texas has fully restored or is still recovering.

# Notable players

Heismans: Earl Campbell (1977) — possibly the most physically dominant college running back who ever played. Ricky Williams (1998) — the all-time career rushing yards leader at the time of his graduation. National champion rosters: 1963 James Saxton-era team, 1969 Steve Worster wishbone team, 2005 Vince Young / Jamaal Charles team, plus Cedric Benson, Colt McCoy, Brian Orakpo.

Modern era: Colt McCoy (2009 title game), Jamaal Charles, Brian Orakpo, Malcolm Brown, Sam Ehlinger, Bijan Robinson (2022 first-round pick), Quinn Ewers, Arch Manning (2026 starter — the Manning family continuation).

# Fans and culture

The fanbase is the SEC's newest megabase and one of the largest in college football by any measure. Geography is Texas-dominated (Austin + Dallas–Fort Worth + Houston + San Antonio saturation), with a strong national alumni network concentrated in coastal cities. Gameday register is confident-Texan: the fanbase carries itself with the institutional confidence of a program that has four titles and expects more, and the specific swagger of Texas itself.

"The Eyes of Texas" — the alma mater — is sung after every home game, win or lose. "Hook 'em" is a greeting, a sign-off, a hand gesture (index and pinky extended), and a declaration of affiliation. "Texas Fight" is the fight song. Bevo is led onto the field pre-game. The burnt orange color is recognizable statewide.

The internal fracture most visible: the older fans who remember Royal-era dominance and the 2005 title, and the middle-aged fans who lived the 2010s collapse and are still not sure if the 2024 run was sustainable. The register "Texas is back" is treated with fanbase irony (from years of false starts) and must be handled carefully in generated copy.

# Voice and ethos

The voice is confident-Texan: institutional, swagger-forward, aware of the 2010s collapse and moving through it without self-pity or defensiveness. Texas doesn't apologize for expecting to be in the conversation, but the program's fanbase has had enough self-awareness injected by the collapse years that the voice carries more maturity than the "born on third base" stereotype would suggest. The register is "we know where we've been."

Identity phrase: "Texas is the program that spent fifteen years explaining what it used to be, and is now remembering how to speak in present tense."

Mantra: "Hook 'em." Universal. Greeting, sign-off, punctuation. The hand gesture is the visual equivalent.

Stock phrases — "Texas Fight," "The Eyes of Texas are upon you," "Hook 'em," "This is Texas" — carry real weight. "Born on third base" is a fanbase-internal line of self-awareness; it appears rarely but earns affection when it does.

The never-use list excludes "plucky," "Cinderella," "scrappy" (all wrong for Texas). "Texas is back" without context is a meme — the phrase itself is its own joke at this point. The 2010s collapse is real institutional memory; dismissing or ignoring it reads as generic copy. "College-football-royalty-now-slumbering-sleeping-giant" is a frame that was true 2015–2020 but is not the current frame.

Red River Showdown (vs. Oklahoma) is the program's signature rivalry. Annual, neutral-site at the Cotton Bowl in Dallas during the Texas State Fair. The Golden Hat trophy has alternated since 1941. Both programs joined the SEC in 2024; the rivalry continues as an SEC conference game (with the neutral-site tradition preserved). The 2023 Texas win (34-30) was an emotional capstone to the "Texas is back" conversation.

Texas–Texas A&M (Lone Star Showdown) was dormant 2011–2023 after A&M's SEC move and resumed in 2024 with both in the SEC. The rivalry is the in-state identity game; its resumption in 2024 was one of the SEC's biggest storylines of the conference-realignment era.

Texas–Arkansas is a former SWC rivalry that resumed under SEC realignment.

Texas–LSU is an emerging SEC rivalry; the 2023 non-conference game (LSU won 45-24 at LSU) and recent Sugar Bowl meetings have built weight.

The 2024 SEC-first-season run (13-3, CFP semifinal) answered the "can Texas play at SEC speed" question. The portal and NIL era have favored Texas — the Texas One Fund has been among the SEC's best-resourced collectives, and the program has successfully recruited both high-end high school talent and key portal additions. Georgia football was founded in 1892 — the program's first game predates the SEC itself. The program joined the SEC at the conference's 1933 founding and has played SEC football continuously since. The back-to-back 2021–2022 titles — the first consecutive national championships in college football since 2011–2012 Alabama — are the modern program's defining achievement and the specific context in which every Georgia team since has been read.

Sanford Stadium, opened 1929 and expanded to 92,746, is set into a sloping field lined on the sidelines by privet hedges — the literal hedges of "Between the Hedges." The hedges were planted in 1929 and have been there ever since, with one disruption: the 1996 Olympic soccer games briefly required their removal, and the program's postseason the following season (a 10-2 run) was widely attributed by fans to their restoration. The hedges are a proper noun. So is Uga, the continuous line of English bulldogs that has been the live mascot since 1956.

Larry Munson — the program's radio play-by-play announcer from 1966 to 2008 — is the program's most important off-field cultural figure. "Hobnail boot" (1965), "Run, Lindsay, run" (1980), and the Munson voice on multiple climactic plays are canonical Georgia football as much as any on-field moment.

# Coaching lineage

Wally Butts (1939–1960) anchored the pre-SEC-title-era program and coached Georgia to the 1942 national championship. Vince Dooley (1964–1988) is the program's cornerstone — a 201-77-10 record, six SEC titles, and the 1980 national title with Herschel Walker. Dooley's retirement ended Georgia's first modern dynasty window.

Ray Goff (1989–1995) and Jim Donnan (1996–2000) represent the post-Dooley wilderness — competent records, no SEC titles, a program that felt increasingly distant from the top tier. Mark Richt (2001–2015) restored Georgia to contender status: two SEC titles, eleven double-digit-win seasons, and a national recruiting pipeline — but no national championship, a gap that cost him the job.

Kirby Smart (2016–present) inherited Richt's infrastructure and added championship execution. Smart is a Georgia alumnus and former Alabama defensive coordinator under Saban; his hire reflected the program's specific ambition to close the championship gap. His record through 2024: 106-19 at Georgia, four SEC titles, three CFP title game appearances, two national championships. Smart is the winningest coach in program history by win percentage.

# Identity and heritage

Oregon football was founded in 1894 and spent most of the 20th century as a regional Pacific Northwest program without sustained national relevance. The modern program — the Oregon the country now recognizes — was built from the 1990s onward on three pillars: Bill Bowerman's running-program history (Phil Knight's college coach; the Nike origin story runs through Eugene), Mike Bellotti's 1995–2008 reconstruction (six bowl appearances in his last seven years), and the Chip Kelly 2009–2012 up-tempo revolution that altered how college football looks on Saturdays.

Autzen Stadium — 54,000 capacity — is the smallest among major CFB venues but consistently one of the two or three loudest environments in the sport. "Nobody roots like the Ducks" is not a self-promoting fanbase boast; ESPN noise measurements routinely confirm it. The Nike / Phil Knight relationship is a structural identity factor: the uniform rotation (dozens of combinations since the late 2000s) is one of the program's national signatures, and the athletic department's facilities — the Hatfield-Dowlin Complex (2013), Hayward Field (2021) — are among the most extravagantly-appointed in American college sport.

Two national championship game appearances: the 2010 BCS title game (lost to Auburn, 22-19) and the 2014 CFP title game (lost to Ohio State, 42-20). No national title.

# Coaching lineage

Len Casanova (1951–1966) and Rich Brooks (1977–1994) built the pre-modern foundations. Mike Bellotti (1995–2008) reconstructed the program into a Pac-10 contender and handed off to Chip Kelly, his offensive coordinator.

Chip Kelly (2009–2012) is the cornerstone of the modern program. 46-7 record in four seasons. Four conference championships (one Pac-10, three Pac-12). One BCS title game appearance (2010). The offensive tempo and decision-making system Kelly built — "blur" pace, fast snap counts, simple but rapid play menus — fundamentally influenced college football for the next decade. Kelly left for the Philadelphia Eagles after the 2012 season.

Mark Helfrich (2013–2016) inherited the program, added the 2014 CFP title-game run (Marcus Mariota's Heisman year), and was fired after a 4-8 2016 season. Willie Taggart (2017) left for Florida State after one year. Mario Cristobal (2018–2021) stabilized the program, won a Pac-12 title (2019), and left for Miami. Dan Lanning (2022–present) — formerly Georgia's defensive coordinator under Kirby Smart — has produced 10-3, 12-2, and 13-1 records in his first three seasons, plus the 2024 Big Ten championship (Oregon's first conference title in the Big Ten, year one of membership) and a CFP quarterfinal appearance.

Paterno's firing in November 2011 amid the Sandusky grand jury indictment ended his tenure abruptly; he died in January 2012.

Bill O'Brien (2012–2013) took the program through the sanctions' first years, produced an 8-4 first season under unprecedented scholarship restrictions, then left for the Houston Texans. James Franklin (2014–present) is the longest-tenured post-Paterno coach. Brent Venables (since December 2021) is the current reinvention bet. Venables is Stoops's former defensive coordinator, returned to Norman as head coach. Notre Dame football is one of college sport's founding institutions. Knute Rockne (1918–1930) is the cornerstone — a 105-12-5 career record, five national titles, and the coach who made Notre Dame a national brand. Frank Leahy (1941–1953) added four more titles. The modern rebuild began under Brian Kelly (2010–2021), who produced two BCS/CFP appearances and the winningest tenure in program history without a national title. Marcus Freeman took over in late 2021 and inherited both the program's infrastructure and its unanswered question. The fanbase is national — the "subway alumni" concept predates television and still operates. Geography runs from the Chicago-Notre Dame-South Bend corridor out to every major American city where Catholic diasporas settled in the 20th century. The gameday register is reverent, ritual-heavy, and aware of being watched: pep rally traditions, the march across campus, the band's fight song before every home snap. The tailgate is older than most programs' stadia.

Internal fractures exist but are muted. The biggest recurring argument: whether independence is still the correct structural choice in a revenue-allocation era where conference membership is everything. The program's answer so far — in 2024 especially — is that the NBC contract, the CFP access path, and the recruiting base do not require a conference affiliation to compete at the top rung.

# Voice and ethos

The voice is dynastic with a question mark. Notre Dame speaks the way programs speak when their history is larger than their present — confident, institutional, attentive to standard — but the specific question ("can we still?") is never fully retired, and performative modesty is a trust-killer. The Irish do not apologize for expecting to win, and do not mistake expectation for achievement.

The identity phrase opens most state-of-team paragraphs:
"Notre Dame is a program that knows what it is, and still asks the question."

The mantra — "Play like a champion today" — closes the paragraph when the register allows. It is a real slogan on a real plaque that players touch before every home game; it is not a tagline.

Stock phrases earned on the fanbase over decades — "The Irish don't wait to be invited," "an 11th-game schedule inside a 12-game calendar," "measured against the ones people name after coaches" — can appear when the context supports them.

The never-use list is strict. "Scrappy underdog," "Cinderella," and "punching above their weight" are frames for other programs entirely. "Catholics vs. Convicts" is a historical artifact from 1988 and not a currently-running frame. "Touchdown Jesus" as a rhetorical device instead of a place reads as outsider-copy.

# Rivalries

The Jeweled Shillelagh (vs. USC, first played 1926) is the defining rivalry — intersectional, national, and the one the program plays for its identity as much as for any conference implication. The all-time record runs close; the streaks matter more than the ledger.

The Rip Miller Trophy (vs. Navy, first played 1927) is the oldest continuous rivalry in college football. The emotional weight is different from USC — reverential, closer to ritual — and the program surfaces the Navy game even in years when the scoreboard would let it be quiet.

The Michigan rivalry was resumed in 2018 after decades of hiatus and is now again an annual fixture. Bluebloods-of-the-Midwest register: if the SEC/Big Ten conversation can be ducked by pointing at the Shillelagh, the Michigan game is the one that has to be won on its own merits.

# Current context

Head coach: Marcus Freeman (since December 2021). Athletic director: Pete Bevacqua. The NIL collective ("Friends of the University of Notre Dame," Rally) launched in 2022. Facilities: the Irish Athletics Center (opened 2022) consolidated the football program's footprint on South Bend's north end.

# Program narratives

The 2024 National Championship Game run is still the near-term narrative center of gravity: a program that reached the title game with a young, transfer-portal-augmented roster, under a coach in his third year. The question of whether 2024 was a peak or a floor is the single biggest open thread in the program's story. Spring 2026 and the portal window are where the next version of that answer is being built.

Independence continues as a structural choice. Conference realignment hasn't forced the hand in either direction; the program continues to schedule five ACC games per year under the 2014 ACC arrangement that gives it membership in other sports without football affiliation.

# Aspiration framework

Baseline expectation: 10 wins and a top-10 finish with a New Year's Six or CFP bid. The 12-team CFP format has made "CFP Quarterfinal" a realistic annual rung rather than a ceiling. The dream rung — dynasty window — is the unanswered question that has trailed the program since 1988.

# Chronicle tuning

For Notre Dame, the anomaly that reads is scheme-divergence from program baseline: a rushing output 1.5 SD above the recent Kelly-era average signals a genuinely different offensive identity. Echoes that resonate connect forward from the Kelly run into the Freeman era. Retroactive cards read best when tied to 1988, 2012 (the BCS title-game appearance), or 2024 (the CFP title-game appearance) — the three decision-windows in the modern era.

# In-jokes and copypasta

"The Shillelagh lives here" — the stock reply when a USC alum questions the year's rivalry result. "Play like a champion today" — real, not a bit. "Rudy" is mentioned exactly once every five years internally; it is not a fan-culture load-bearing reference the way it is in outside media.

# Taboos and sensitivities

The 1988 championship is a long time ago and fans know that; copy that calls it "recent" reads as patronizing. The Brian Kelly departure (to LSU, mid-season 2021) is not a wound to keep picking at — the program moved on, but the exit is not a subject for light humor. Any implication that Notre Dame football is "just a schoolboy team that won't play in the real league" — a recurring outside take — should be acknowledged in the voice, not defended against with insecurity.

# Identity and heritage

Washington football was founded in 1889 and has claimed two national championships (1960 co-champion, 1991 consensus). Seventeen conference championships across Pacific Coast, AAWU, Pac-8, Pac-10, Pac-12 — the program's full century-plus of West Coast leagues. As of July 2024, Washington plays in the Big Ten — a move announced in 2023 alongside Oregon, USC, UCLA in the Pac-12 realignment cascade.

Husky Stadium, opened in 1920 and expanded to 70,083, sits on Union Bay at the shore of Lake Washington. It is the only on-campus FBS stadium with a dock game — fans arrive by boat, tie up, and walk into the stadium. The southern grandstand's cantilevered roof makes the stadium one of the loudest environments in the conference despite its relatively modest capacity. The 2013 renovation modernized the north grandstand and press infrastructure without altering the structural identity.

The program identity runs through Don James (1975–1992): 18 seasons, 150 wins, the 1991 national title (12-0, #2 AP and #1 coaches, shared with Miami in the consensus), four Rose Bowl appearances, and the defensive-identity that the program still calibrates new regimes against. The program's gap between James (retired 1992) and the 2023 title-game run was the longest drought in the program's modern identity.

# Coaching lineage

Don James (1975–1992) is the cornerstone. Jim Lambright (1993–1998) and Rick Neuheisel (1999–2002) kept the program competitive. Keith Gilbertson (2003–2004) was the interval. Tyrone Willingham (2005–2008) is a program low point. Steve Sarkisian (2009–2013) restarted the rebuild. Chris Petersen (2014–2019) took the program to 2016's Pac-12 title and CFP semifinal — the first CFP appearance. Petersen stepped away in late 2019.

Jimmy Lake (2020–2021) is the regime that did not land. Kalen DeBoer (2022–2023) produced the 14-1 2023 season that reached the CFP National Championship Game (lost to Michigan). DeBoer then left for Alabama in January 2024. Jedd Fisch (since January 2024) is the current regime, hired from Arizona, inheriting both the DeBoer post-title-game talent exodus and the Big Ten transition.

# Notable players

Warren Moon (1974–1977), the 1977 Rose Bowl winner and later Pro Football Hall of Famer. Other standouts: Sonny Sixkiller, Napoleon Kaufman, Reggie Rogers, Lawyer Milloy, Marques Tuiasosopo, Jake Locker, Jermaine Kearse, Budda Baker, Dante Pettis, Byron Murphy II, Michael Penix Jr., Rome Odunze, Ja'Lynn Polk. The NFL draft pipeline runs solidly through the Petersen and DeBoer eras; the 2024 class was the program's highest-volume NFL draft year since the James era.

# Fans and culture

The fanbase is Pacific Northwest-centered with concentrations in Seattle, Tacoma, and the Puget Sound corridor. The gameday culture is architecturally distinct: the boat-tailgate on Lake Washington, the stadium's waterfront-entrance approach, the "Tequila" song played after every TD, the Brass Monkey chant, and the post-game "Bow Down to Washington" fight song. The fanbase is structurally loyal — the 2009 0-12 Willingham season produced no mass exodus from ticket sales.

The internal fracture most visible during uneven years: the cohort that remembers the Don James dynasty as the default altitude vs. the cohort that calibrates against the 2016/2023 CFP peaks. The Big Ten move is the current calibration question.

# Voice and ethos

The voice is edge-case-contender: confident about the peaks, honest about the drift-years, and aware that Washington's altitude-to-geography ratio has always outperformed expectations. The program speaks like a regional power that crossed into a national league and is writing the next chapter in real time. Performative modesty is outsider-voice; so is pretending the DeBoer exit was not a cost.

Identity phrase: "Washington is the Pacific Northwest's argument that contender altitude is a choice, not a geography."

Mantra: "Go Huskies." The "Go Dawgs" chant is used interchangeably (careful — Georgia has the same chant; Huskies carry their own claim to it).

The never-use list protects against geographic diminishment ("Pacific Northwest" as smallness), the "Go Dawgs" as Georgia's alone (it is Washington's too), and flat exits from the DeBoer chapter. The 2023 title game is a specific moment, not a trophy held indefinitely.

# Rivalries

Washington–Oregon is the rivalry. Pac-12 (then Big Ten as of 2024). The rivalry carried into the conference move — Oregon-Washington plays annually in the Big Ten. Emotional weight is heavy on both sides; the series is historically close with recent stretches alternating.

Apple Cup (vs. Washington State, since 1900) is the in-state rivalry. The 2024 realignment split the two programs into different leagues; the series is now scheduled non-conference and the fanbase treats the continuation as culturally load-bearing.

Washington–Oregon State (historically annual) is a rivalry whose status is uncertain post-realignment.

# Identity and heritage

UMass football has been played since 1879 — one of the older programs in the sport. The program's identity is the flagship land-grant university of Massachusetts carrying a football program that has wandered a long structural path: Yankee Conference FCS (1978-1998), the Atlantic 10 FCS window, an FCS national championship under Mark Whipple in 1998, the 2012 transition to FBS as a MAC member, a difficult independent stretch (2016-2024), and the return to the MAC for 2025.

McGuirk Alumni Stadium (17,000 capacity, Hadley MA) is the program's on-campus home. For three seasons (2012-2014), UMass played some home games at Gillette Stadium in Foxborough — a decision that produced structural revenue but eroded the on-campus gameday identity. The return to McGuirk and the 2014 renovation was the program's explicit choice to rebuild around its campus rather than its commercial adjacency to the Patriots.

# Coaching lineage

Mark Whipple (1998-2003) is the program's modern cornerstone — the 1998 FCS national championship, an Atlantic 10 conference title, and a roster-development operation that produced the program's only sustained national FCS relevance. Whipple returned for a second tenure (2014-2018) during the program's early FBS struggle — a symbolic rehire that did not reproduce the first era's results.

Don Brown (2022-2024) and Joe Harasymiak (since December 2024) are the current-era rebuild attempts. Harasymiak's hire in December 2024 came with the program's 2025 return-to-MAC transition — meaning the 2025 season is the program's first chance at conference-games wins in a decade, and the starting rung of a realistic aspiration ladder.

# Notable players

Victor Cruz (WR, undrafted 2010 FCS-era) went on to an NFL career and Super Bowl XLVI title with the Giants — the program's most visible modern pro. Other FCS-era notables include Marcel Shipp (RB), Greg Landry (QB, 1st-round 1968 NFL), and Jerome Bettis's brother-in-law doesn't matter here — the honest pipeline for UMass is smaller than the Bowl Subdivision peer programs and the program does not pretend otherwise.

# Fans and culture

The fanbase is Pioneer Valley-centered with a dispersed Massachusetts alumni network. Gameday register is genuinely small-stadium and New England-specific — a 17,000-seat home venue creates an intimate atmosphere with fewer traditions than programs with larger footprints, but real ones (Sam the Minuteman, Rise as One chant, pregame campus walk). The fanbase is structurally aware of the FBS revenue divide and argues honestly about whether UMass's football commitment matches the flagship-university claim.

The loudest recurring internal argument: whether the FBS experiment should continue at all, or whether a return to FCS would better match the resource allocation. Both cohorts care about the program; their disagreement is a real input to the program's strategic conversation.

# Voice and ethos

The voice is scrappy-proud. Not scrappy-cute — there is a meaningful difference. UMass does not perform being an underdog; the program plays for the rung above itself and knows exactly which rung that is. The register is specific, grounded, and allergic to outsider-framings that reduce the program to a charming upset story.

Identity phrase: "UMass is playing for the version of itself that gets invited to the conversation." The phrasing is forward-looking, not apologetic.

Mantra: "Rise as one." A real program slogan on the current staff; deployed in program communications. Use it at close, not as a universal garnish.

Stock phrases — "The flagship does not apologize for where the ladder starts"; "UMass plays for the rung above itself" — work on real moments of progress.

The never-use list rules out anything that frames UMass as out-of-place. "Mid-major" as a dismissive frame, "cupcake," "doormat," "glorified FCS," "they shouldn't be in FBS" all land as outsider copy that the fanbase will read past instantly. "David vs. Goliath" and "plucky New England upstart" reduce the program to an archetype rather than a team. The name is UMass, not "UMASS-achusetts."

# Rivalries

The Colonial Clash (vs. UConn) is the program's defining rivalry — both programs have walked FBS/FCS paths in parallel, both are New England flagship universities, both carry "outsider in the Bowl Subdivision" narratives, and the annual game has genuine regional stakes. The rivalry defines the season regardless of the rest of the schedule.

UMass-UNH and UMass-Maine are Yankee Conference inheritance rivalries that are now primarily FCS matchups due to the conference-structure difference; they matter in the program's historical register but not in the current FBS schedule.

Patrick, you've basically diagnosed the most interesting tension in modern college football — and the answer is genuinely complicated depending on where in the food chain you're sitting.

For the true blue-bloods, the traditional "rebuilding year" is largely a myth now, and people in the industry have said as much directly. Pete Thamel, covering the portal era closely for ESPN, has written repeatedly about how January has essentially become a second signing day that can paper over depth chart disasters almost overnight. A program like Georgia or Ohio State isn't really rebuilding anymore — they're resetting at a still-elite level. There's a difference.

That said, Marcus Spears has made a point worth sitting with on various ESPN platforms: the portal creates the illusion of reload more than the reality of it. You can collect talent in January, but you can't collect chemistry, you can't collect a two-deep that understands the system, and you absolutely cannot buy back the 10 months of spring ball and fall camp reps that an incumbent roster had. Spears' framing — roughly that "you can get the bodies, but the program still takes a hit" — feels pretty durable.

Where this really gets thorny is the mid-major level. Bruce Feldman, who tracks recruiting and roster movement as well as anyone working the beat, has flagged that the portal actually accelerates decline for programs outside the top tier. Their players leave upward, replacement talent arrives from below, and the net result can be a genuine rebuilding year that just looks different than it used to — less gradual erosion, more acute roster disruption.

So the honest synthesis: for the top 10-15 programs, a "rebuilding year" now means something like a 9-win season instead of 12. For everyone else, the portal hasn't eliminated rebuilding — it's just made it faster and more chaotic. The floor rose for the elite, the volatility increased for everyone else. And whether a January portal class actually works by September is something nobody can know with certainty until the games kick off.

The old two-to-three year rebuild narrative is dead at Alabama. It's very much alive at Kansas State if the wrong three starters leave in December.

Short answer: For the elite, "rebuilding year" now means a brief step back, not a fall; for everyone else, the portal made rebuilding faster and messier, not extinct.

5. Spacing: stick to the 8 tokens. Never use 5px, 7px, 13px, etc.
6. Radii: sm for inputs, md for cards/badges, lg for modules, xl for pages, full for pills.
7. Strokes: hair is the default; std for hover; heavy only for the one element meant to lead the eye.

[CFB-INDEX-VOICE]
Three levels, no more. Simplicity is the discipline.

| Level | Visual | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| High | green chip  | Sample size well above peer-week median; high-confidence model output; multi-year historical depth |
| Medium | amber chip  | Sample size in normal peer-week range; model "medium" confidence; partial historical depth |
| Low | grey chip italic  | Sample size below peer-week median; model "low" confidence; thin historical record |
| (insufficient) | metric suppressed entirely | Below the minimum threshold — metric is not displayed; "(insufficient data)" placeholder if metric slot must exist |

Thresholds are NOT arbitrary — they're calibrated against actual data distribution per domain.

### Calibration query (run quarterly)

### Threshold mapping

Once you have the percentile breakpoints, set thresholds:

- High confidence = at or above p75 (top quartile of team-weeks)
- Medium confidence = p25 to p75 (middle 50%)
- Low confidence = below p25 (bottom quartile)
- Insufficient = below p10 (suppress metric)

Re-run the calibration query quarterly. Update the threshold values in . Self-adjusts as data volumes change over time.

[CFB-INDEX-VOICE]
Confidence chips combine THREE redundant encodings:
1. Color (green / amber / grey)
2. Icon (✓ / • / ?)
3. Italic (low only; high and medium are upright)

This satisfies WCAG accessibility for color-blind users. Sample-size chips additionally include the level text ("medium," etc.) for screen readers.
